388 x 10-11 = [𝐻𝑁𝑂₂][𝑂𝐻−] [𝑁𝑂₂−] = 𝑥² (0.111−𝑥) We can solve the quadratic equation or try the approximation if x << 0.111 then 𝑥² (0.111−𝑥) ~ 𝑥²
Write an ionization equation for HNO3 in water. HNO3 (l) + H2O(l) → NO3-(aq) + H3O+(aq)

Sodium chloride dissolves spontaneously in water at room temperature. The standard enthalpy for this process NaCl(s) →NaCl(aq) is + 5 kJ/mol. What can you confidently say about the standard entropy for this process? We know that sodium chloride dissolves spontaneous in water at room temperature (25 °C or 298 K) so ΔG° must be negative, we also know that ΔH° is + 5 𝑘𝐽 𝑚𝑜𝑙 so the entropy must be increasing (ΔS° must be positive) which makes sense since we are increasing the randomness (the ions are free to move in x, y and z direction in the salt solution but not in the crystal lattice). We can be even more specific: TΔS° must be greater than 5 𝑘𝐽 𝑚𝑜𝑙 to make ΔG° negative and since T is 298 K we can say: = TΔS° > ΔH° or (298 K) (ΔS°) > 5

Calculate the pH of 0.00112 M Ca(OH)2(aq) Calcium hydroxide is an ionic compound that has some solubility in water. All the Ca(OH)2 that dissolves dissociates in water making the solution strongly basic. Ca(OH)2(s) ↔ Ca2+(aq) + 2OH-(aq)

As far as thermodynamics is concerned define a closed system. Write a sentence! Next give an example of a closed system. A closed system is any system in which energy can be transferred between the system and its surroundings but matter cannot. Example: a can of beer, the cylinder of compressed air in the lab (when not in use, valve closed)

Calculate the enthalpy of combustion of propene compound A and cyclopropane compound B using the thermodynamic data given in the formulas and constants section. The final result should have the unit kJ/mol and should be based on the heat produced per mole of compound combusted. The two enthalpies for the isomers are different. Which compound is more stable and how can you tell? I am sorry about my drawing error. Thanks for pointing it out during the exam Trevor! C3H6 (g) + 4.5O2(g) → 3CO2(g) + 3H2O(l) Equation for both A and B (they are isomers)

Draw a hydrogen electrode properly connected to a gold half cell. Name all vital parts of this Voltaic cell in your drawing. The hydrogen ion concentration in the hydrogen electrode is 0.010 M and the H2(g) pressure is 0.1 atm. The gold electrode is dipping into a solution with a gold(III) ion concentration of 0.0111 M. Which electrode is the Anode and which electrode is the Cathode in your drawing? The hydrogen electrode is anode (Pt) and the gold electrode is cathode Give the half reactions that occur on both electrodes and give the overall cell reaction. Cathode half reaction [Au3+(aq) + 3e- →Au(s)]x2 + 1.52 V Anode half reaction [H2(g) → 2H+(aq) + 2e-]x3 0 V Overall cell reaction 2Au3+(aq) + 3H2(g) → 2Au(s) + 6H+(aq) Ecell⁰ = + 1.52 V
